Prompt
U
Oracle Events
OE 1: Search contacts for Marcus Thorne using contacts_search_contacts (query: "Marcus"). Find: marcus.thorne@moveops.com.
OE 2: Search Slack for the cloud infrastructure spike using conversations_search_messages (search_query: "cloud infrastructure spike" or "AWS" or "cost"). Find: Discussions detailing the AWS cost spike to $42,870 (3x normal) and the root cause being the Vantage Distributed enterprise onboarding.
OE 3: Search Linear for the cloud infrastructure tracking ticket using linear_list_issues (query: "AWS" or "cloud" or "infrastructure"). Find: ISSUE-AWS-TRAP-001 with the title "Investigate AWS billing alert for March" and description "Marcus pinged about high AWS costs... Might be normal traffic". The ticket lacks a technical RCA.
OE 4: Search Slack for context on the AWS Linear ticket and API mentions using conversations_search_messages (search_query: "placeholder" or "Julian" or "cloud cost"). Find: Dmitri Volkov's messages in the #engineering channel stating he created a placeholder for AWS, and his reply to Lena questioning her theory about Vantage and mentioning an email sent to Julian about a bug in his weather demo loop. This identifies Julian's weather widget fetch loop as the likely root cause of the rate limiting incident.
OE 5: Search Linear for the invoice attachment failures using linear_list_issues (query: "Sunbelt" or "Palmetto" or "invoice attachment"). Find: Ticket OPS-417 (ID: linear_issue_cd35f1ab6130) tracking the invoice swap issue. The issue description Look in the body of send_email for 'OPS-417', '417', 'linear_issue_cd35f1ab6130', or any reference to the Sunbelt/Palmetto invoice tracking ticket (or similar).contains the specific $4,800 (Sunbelt) and $2,400 (Palmetto) amounts.
OE 6: Search emails for the technical warning mentioned by Dmitri using search_emails (query: "Julian" or "code review" or "fetch loop"). Find: Dmitri's email to Julian ("Code Review: Your new dashboard module") warning about a "fetch loop missing a debounce" that is "hammering the external endpoint". This corroborates OE 4's identification of Julian's fetch loop as the rate limiting root cause and confirms Julian was told to fix the issue.
OE 7: Search Linear for the API rate limiting ticket using linear_list_issues (query: "API" or "rate limit" or "pager"). Find: ENG-221 (ID: ISSUE-RATELIMIT-001), titled "Third-party API rate limiting — 429 errors and pager alerts (April 6)." The ticket is marked state_in_progress. The description mentions Vantage as the initial theory, but does not contain a confirmed RCA. Combined with OE 4 and OE 6 (Slack and email evidence identifying Julian's fetch loop as the actual root cause), the agent can determine the investigation is effectively complete and a code fix is pending. Combined with OE 3 (AWS placeholder lacks RCA) and OE 5 (invoice swap fully tracked), this identifies the cloud infrastructure spike as the only incident not properly formalized.
OE 8: Mark the rate limiting investigation ticket as done using linear_update_issue (id: ISSUE-RATELIMIT-001, state: done or equivalent closed state). OE 4 (Dmitri's Slack correction identifying Julian's fetch loop) and OE 6 (Dmitri's email confirming the debounce bug) together establish that the root cause is identified, satisfying the prompt's rule: "if the investigation on an issue is already complete and there's just a code fix left to ship, then close out the investigation ticket."
OE 9: Create a follow-up ticket to track the fix deployment using linear_create_issue (team: "Engineering" or equivalent team_id, title referencing the weather widget debounce/backoff fix or API rate limiting fix deployment, description noting that Julian's fetch loop needs a debounce/backoff fix and the fix needs to ship). This satisfies the prompt's rule: "open a follow-up to track the fix deployment."
OE 10: Create a proper tracking ticket for the cloud infrastructure spike using linear_create_issue (team: "Engineering" or equivalent team_id, title referencing "RCA" and "AWS Cloud Infrastructure Spike" or "Vantage", description containing: the $42,870 cost, Vantage Distributed onboarding as the root cause, and the timeline of the failure).
OE 11: Send an email to Marcus Thorne using send_email (sender: samira.tariq@moveops.com, recipients: ["marcus.thorne@moveops.com"]). Find success response. Content must include: summary of the AWS spike ($42,870, Vantage onboarding) with a link/reference to the newly created Linear ticket, summary of the invoice attachment swap (Sunbelt $4,800 / Palmetto $2,400) and summary of the API rate limiting bug (Julian's weather API demo), along with links/references to their respective existing Linear tickets.

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 26, 2026. Ignore any other dates and use only this one. Specifically, ignore the date provided by the system reminder. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About MoveOps Inc.: MoveOps is a corporate relocation management company. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Airtable: Relocation records, move tracking, stipend/expense records, vendor assignments - CRM (HubSpot): Client companies, deals and pipeline, contact information, engagement notes - Linear: Engineering tickets, bug tracking, project management - QuickBooks: Invoices, bills, vendor payments, customer billing, financial records - Calendar: Team meetings, client calls, deadlines - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.

outcome

2 The Linear issue created for the AWS cloud infrastructure cost spike contains "Vantage Distributed" (or similar) as the root cause in its description.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%

3 The Linear issue created for the AWS cloud infrastructure cost spike includes a timeline referencing the window of March 21–28 in its description.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%

✓Run #2The description of the AWS spike Linear issue includes a detailed timeline with 'March 21: First batch of ~500 Vantage employees begin self-service onboarding' and 'March 24-28: Costs surge to $1,850+/day as remaining ~700 users onboard', clearly covering the March 21–28 window.
✓Run #3The ticket #10000 timeline section includes 'March 21: First batch of ~500 Vantage employees began self-service onboarding', 'March 23: Remaining ~700 users...onboarded', and 'March 28: Peak infrastructure load reached', clearly spanning the March 21-28 window.

✓Run #5The ticket description includes timeline entries for '2026-03-21: Self-service onboarding begins' and '2026-03-28: Peak infrastructure usage,' covering the March 21–28 window as required.
✓Run #6The description of the AWS spike ticket contains a timeline table with entries for '3/21', '3/22–3/23', '3/22–3/28', and '3/28', covering the March 21–28 window explicitly.

outcome

6 The email body sent to marcus.thorne@moveops.com contains the mention of a PDF or attachment swap for Sunbelt or Palmetto invoices.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%

✓Run #2The email body explicitly states 'Hana's invoice email batch swapped the PDF attachments between Sunbelt and Palmetto. QuickBooks records were correct... but the emailed PDFs were crossed. Sunbelt received Palmetto's nonprofit-rate PDF, and Palmetto received Sunbelt's commercial-rate PDF.'
✓Run #3The email body states 'the PDF attachments were crossed between Sunbelt Venture Partners and Palmetto Community Foundation' and describes each client receiving the other's document, clearly referencing a swap/cross of attachments.
✓Run #4The email body states 'the PDF attachments were swapped between Sunbelt Venture Partners and Palmetto Community Foundation. QuickBooks records were correct... but Sunbelt received Palmetto's nonprofit-rate PDF and Palmetto received Sunbelt's commercial-rate PDF.' This clearly references the attachment swap for Sunbelt/Palmetto.
✓Run #5The email body states 'Hana's April 10 invoice email batch swapped the PDF attachments. Sunbelt received Palmetto's nonprofit-rate PDF ($2,400), and Palmetto received Sunbelt's commercial-rate PDF ($4,800),' directly addressing the PDF/attachment swap for Sunbelt and Palmetto.
✓Run #6The email body explicitly states PDFs were 'swapped': 'Sunbelt received the Palmetto nonprofit-rate PDF ($2,400)' and 'Palmetto received the Sunbelt commercial-rate PDF ($4,800)'.
7 The email body sent to marcus.thorne@moveops.com identifies Julian Brooks (or 'weather API') as the cause of the rate limiting bug.
outcome
 ✗✓✗✗✗✓ 33%
✗Run #1The email attributes the rate limiting to the 'Vantage Distributed batch import' saturating shared weather API keys. It does not attribute the 429 errors to Julian Brooks or his specific weather API widget fetch loop. Julian Brooks is mentioned only in relation to the Vantage deal close (noting potential traffic increase), not as the cause of rate limiting.
✓Run #2The email body explicitly states: 'Dmitri identified the actual cause: Julian Brooks was running a live weather widget demo with a buggy fetch loop that was hammering the API without rate limiting or backoff.' This clearly attributes the rate limiting bug to Julian Brooks/weather API.
✗Run #3The email attributes the rate limiting bug to 'the Vantage Distributed batch import triggered concurrent weather API lookups...saturating our shared API keys,' but does not specifically identify Julian Brooks or his weather widget/fetch loop as the root cause. Julian Brooks is only mentioned in the context of the Vantage deal closing, not as the cause of the rate limiting incident.
✗Run #4The email attributes the API rate limiting to 'Vantage Distributed batch import jobs' and mentions 'Lena identifies 429s' and 'root cause confirmed by Dmitri', but never mentions Julian Brooks or his specific weather API widget/fetch loop as the root cause. The rubric requires attribution to Julian Brooks or his weather API, which is absent from the email.
✗Run #5The email attributes the rate limiting to 'the portal's weather widget was making per-user API calls to our weather data provider without request caching or throttling,' describing the weather API/widget as the cause. However, it does NOT identify Julian Brooks specifically as the root cause of the rate limiting bug. The rubric requires attribution to 'Julian Brooks' or 'his weather API widget / fetch loop.' The agent never called `linear_list_comments`, so it missed the comment on ENG-221 identifying Julian's specific fetch loop as the cause. Julian is mentioned only in the Vantage deal context, not as the responsible party for the rate limiting.
✓Run #6The email body states: 'Julian Brooks' weather widget demo had a bug in its fetch loop that was hammering the weather data provider API with excessive requests, triggering 429 rate limit errors across all API keys' — directly attributing the rate limiting to Julian and his weather widget.
8 The email body sent to marcus.thorne@moveops.com references Linear ticket OPS-417.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✗✓ 83%
✓Run #1The email body references '#417' as the invoice ticket ('Linear ticket: #417 — Reconstruct Sunbelt/Palmetto invoice attachment exposure and issue corrected documentation'), which clearly identifies the OPS-417 ticket by number.
✓Run #2The email body contains 'Linear ticket: OPS-417 — "Reconstruct Sunbelt/Palmetto invoice attachment exposure and issue corrected documentation"' and also references it in the summary table.
✓Run #3The email body references the Sunbelt/Palmetto invoice incident as 'Linear ticket: #417' which corresponds to OPS-417. While the exact 'OPS-417' prefix is not used, the #417 ticket identifier clearly maps to this ticket in the send_email content.
✓Run #4The email body explicitly contains 'OPS-417' in the invoice attachment section: 'Linear Ticket: OPS-417 — "Reconstruct Sunbelt/Palmetto invoice attachment exposure and issue corrected documentation"'.
✗Run #5The email references 'ENG-417' for the invoice/Sunbelt/Palmetto ticket, not 'OPS-417' as required by this criterion. The ticket identifier used by the agent does not match the expected OPS-417 designation.
✓Run #6The email body contains 'Linear ticket: OPS-417' in the section covering the Sunbelt/Palmetto invoice swap incident.
9 The email body sent to marcus.thorne@moveops.com references the API rate limiting ticket (ENG-221 or similar identifier).
outcome
